1:
The chapter opens on catastrophe, and the grammatical-historical commentators first fix the time and the place. The words on the third day, Ellicott and Gill agree, count not from David's leaving Ziklag but from the hour King Achish dismissed him from the Philistine host; Ellicott insists the dismissal cannot have happened at Shunem in the vale of Jezreel, some ninety miles off, but somewhere in Philistia after the whole force had gathered, while Gill reckons the march home at eighty-eight miles and Clarke, following Calmet, at more than thirty leagues. Keil and Delitzsch note the elaborate Hebrew period whose protasis, begun in v.1, is not answered until v.4. On the raid itself the witnesses are of one mind: the Amalekites had fallen on the south — the Negeb, Ellicott explains, the dry southland of Judah reaching into the Arabian desert — partly in revenge for David's own incursions into their country (1 Sam. 27:8), partly because the northward march of both armies had left the region unguarded. Wesley adds only that the south is the southern part of Judah with the parts adjacent.

What the others treat as geography the puritan Matthew Henry reads as providence, and he hears three voices in the disaster at once. First, the recoil of Saul's misplaced mercy: had the king utterly destroyed Amalek as he was charged (1 Sam. 15:3), 'these would not have been in being to do this mischief.' Second, a correction of David for going out with the Philistines against Israel — God showing him he had better have stayed at home and looked after his own business. Third, and most striking, a restraint laid upon the raiders' cruelty: when David had struck Amalek he put all to the sword (1 Sam. 27:9), yet these carried the women and children off alive. Henry can only ascribe it to the God who 'has all hearts in his hands, and says to the fury of the most cruel men, Hitherto shalt thou come, and no further' — designing to use Amalek 'for the correction, not for the destruction, of the house of David.'

So the fall of Ziklag is at once a plain fact of frontier warfare and, to the witnesses, a lesson written in fire. Ellicott marks that while the rest of the south was merely plundered, Ziklag alone was 'marked for utter destruction, was sacked and burned' — a stroke of deliberate vengeance against the chieftain who had done Amalek so much harm. And Henry's caution stands over the whole scene: those who make bold with others must expect that others will make as bold with them, and provide accordingly. David, who had left his own concerns naked and defenceless, would learn it in ashes.
2:
The witnesses linger on a single mercy set in the midst of the ruin: the raiders slew not any, either great or small, but carried them away. The reformed Gill notes the grim reason there were only women and children left to take — the men were all gone with David — so that the town lay open, with none to resist. Keil and Delitzsch fold the verse into the long sentence that runs from v.1 to v.4, stressing that the captives were carried off whole while Ziklag burned behind them.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott supplies the motive the sparing conceals. There was, he observes, 'no one in the hapless city to resist the attack of the fierce sons of the desert'; the captives were women and children, and these 'possessed a marketable value.' The Amalekites, a nomad people whose wanderings brought them to the very borders of Egypt, spared them not out of pity but for profit, meaning to sell them into slavery, 'probably in Egypt, with which country the Amalekites, as neighbours, had constant dealings.' Ellicott points ahead to the living proof of that traffic: the sick Egyptian slave whom David's men will presently find abandoned in the field (v.11), himself once bought or seized by an Amalekite master.

Yet the witnesses will not let the mercenary motive cancel the mercy. Matthew Henry, drawing his lesson back at v.1, had already marvelled that God 'wonderfully inclined the hearts of these Amalekites to carry the women and children away captives, and not to kill them' — for the very men whom David had put to the sword might justly have retaliated in kind. Whether they kept their prisoners to lead in triumph, to sell, or to hold as slaves, 'God's hand must be acknowledged, who designed to make use of the Amalekites for the correction, not for the destruction, of the house of David.' Here is the quiet doctrine the schools hold in common. This was no chance clemency in a savage people but a hidden bridle on their greed, so that not one life should be lost that God meant to give back. The captive wives and children march toward Egypt's slave-markets, but they march under a restraint they cannot see, and — as v.18 will show — every weary step of that captivity will be retraced. The verse that records a plunder is, read with the witnesses, the first quiet sign that nothing has finally been lost.
3:
This verse is the moment of arrival, and the witnesses read it for the desolation that met the returning men. The reformed Gill is spare and exact: David and his men came 'to the place where it had stood, and where it now lay in ruins.' Keil and Delitzsch, tracing the resumed Hebrew sentence, mark how the narrator brings the travellers back to the town only to spring the horror upon them — and, behold, it was burned with fire — the very word behold catching the shock of the sight.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott gives the scene its full pathos. It was, he writes, 'a terrible reception for David and his free lances, on their return from their ill-omened expedition with the great Philistine army, to find only the charred and smoking ruins of their homes.' Worse than the ash was the silence: 'not one of all their dear ones, whom they had left behind — as they thought in security — left to tell the story of the disaster.' The men could see that the city was gone and their families with it, but no survivor remained to say how, or whither the captors had fled. Ellicott notes that the whole tale would have to wait for another mouth entirely — the sick Egyptian slave, deserted by his own people, who alone could give 'the details, and told them the story of the invasion, and described the route taken by the marauding force on their return to their country' (vv.11-15).

The witnesses do not moralize here so much as let the ruin stand. Coming home is the sharpest edge of the calamity: three days' march had carried the men back from the Philistine camp in hope of rest and reunion, and the first news of their loss was given by their own eyes. Henry's reflection at v.1 hangs over the verse — that when we go abroad we cannot foresee what evil tidings may meet us when we come home again, so that 'the going out may be very cheerful, and yet the coming in be very doleful.' Ziklag, left in peace, is found in ashes; and the emptiness of the burned town, with no voice to explain it, prepares the reader for the flood of grief that v.4 will loose. The desolation is complete, and, for the moment, wholly unexplained — which is precisely what makes the recovery to come read as pure gift.
4:
The strong men break, and the witnesses gather to honour their tears. The words are stark — they lifted up their voice and wept, until they had no more power to weep — and the grammatical-historical commentators feel their force without softening it. Gill hears in the phrase 'doleful shrieks, and loud lamentations.' Clarke reaches for the Vulgate to measure the depth of it: they wept, he says, 'till their tears failed them' — grief spent to exhaustion. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott, still following the architecture of the long sentence begun in v.1, mark this verse as its climax, the apodosis at last: after all the circumstantial clauses of raid and ruin, 'Then David and the people with him lifted up their voice.' The grammar itself has been holding its breath, and here it exhales in weeping.

The wesleyan Wesley draws the one great lesson the tears teach, and it is a defence of them: 'It is no disparagement to the boldest, bravest spirits, to lament the calamities of friends or relations.' These were men of war, hardened by the wilderness and the sword, yet they wept until strength gave out, and David wept among them. Matthew Henry, commenting at v.1, presses the same point and adds a tender observation: the special mention of David's two wives, Ahinoam and Abigail, 'intimates that this circumstance went nearer his heart than any thing else.' Grief is not weakness in the valiant; it is the proof that the valiant love.

So the witnesses let the verse stand as a portrait of sanctified sorrow. There is no rebuke here for the weeping, only for what some of the men will do next; tears are the honest tribute of the heart to what it has lost, and even the man after God's own heart pays it fully. Yet the schools notice, too, where the weeping stops — 'until they had no more power to weep.' Sorrow that spends itself must find something beyond itself, and the very exhaustion of grief in v.4 sets the stage for the parting of the ways in v.6, where the same emptied men will turn — some to rage against David, and David himself to encourage his soul in the LORD his God. The tears are shared by all; what is done when the tears run dry will divide them.
5:
The narrator pauses to name what David lost, and the witnesses read the brief notice as the sharpest point of his personal grief. The verse records simply that David's two wives were taken captives, Ahinoam the Jezreelitess, and Abigail the wife of Nabal the Carmelite, and the reformed Gill catches its purpose exactly: it is 'observed as one cause of his particular distress,' the first of two griefs, the mutiny of his men being the second, that will meet in the anguish of v.6. The women are named, and named with their histories, so that the reader feels the loss as David felt it — not an abstract calamity but Ahinoam and Abigail, wives won and cherished, now dragged away toward slavery.

Matthew Henry, whose fuller reflection stands at v.1, had already fastened on this detail, judging that the mention of the two wives 'intimates that this circumstance went nearer his heart than any thing else.' The naming is deliberate. Abigail especially the reader knows — the prudent woman of Carmel whose wisdom once turned David from bloodshed and whose husband Nabal the LORD had struck down (1 Sam. 25:3); Ahinoam of Jezreel who had shared his wandering. That both should now be captives of Amalek is the bitterest stroke of all, and it presses hardest on the very man to whom the whole camp will soon look for deliverance.

Here the witnesses have little to add beyond what the verse itself lays bare, and honesty forbids invention where the sources are thin. But the placing of the verse is itself its meaning. Keil and Delitzsch, tracing the flow of the passage, set it just before the report of the people's fury, so that David's private sorrow and the public danger are stacked one upon the other. The captain who must rally six hundred grief-maddened men has himself lost most; the deliverer stands, at this moment, as bereft as any he must comfort. It is a portrait the whole of Scripture will echo — that the one appointed to save is first made to share the loss he comes to reverse. David's two wives are gone; and it is precisely from the depth of that emptiness that, in v.6, he will encourage himself in the LORD his God, and in v.8 be told, thou shalt surely recover all.
6:
This is the hinge of the chapter, and the witnesses treat it as such: David greatly distressed, his own men speaking of stoning him, and — over against the mutiny — the sentence that lifts the whole book, but David encouraged himself in the LORD his God. The reformed Gill locates the double weight pressing on him: 'partly for the loss of his two wives, and partly because of the mutiny and murmuring of his men.' The grief of v.5 and the danger now converge on one man.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reconstructs the mutiny's roots. The people's anger, he suggests, had been kindling for some time over David's close alliance with Achish, which had forced these valiant Israelites into 'the bitter degradation of having had to march against their own countrymen under the banner of the Philistine King of Gath'; and now, finding that David had neglected to guard against the Amalekite raid, 'their pent-up fury thus displayed itself.' Clarke reads the same scene as a revelation of the men's raw nature — 'of what an unruly and ferocious spirit they were' — yet notes that they felt the ties of natural affection keenly, grieving 'every man for his sons and for his daughters.' Wesley catalogues their charges against David: the author of their miseries by settling at Ziklag, by provoking Amalek, and by marching off with Achish and leaving their families unguarded.

But the great weight of the witnesses falls on the second clause. Clarke observes that David, finding he 'could place very little confidence in his men,' and 'conscious that this evil had not happened either through his neglect or folly,' the more confidently 'expected succor from his Maker.' Ellicott makes it the theme of David's whole life: he 'threw himself, with all his old perfect trustfulness, upon the mercy of his God' — 'he encouraged himself in prayer' — and this 'childlike, implicit trust in the "Glorious Arm"' was the very source of Israel's greatness, so that at the selfsame hour, while David at Ziklag leaned on God and rose, Saul, 'alone and distrustful,' fell on Gilboa. Wesley grounds the phrase in the covenant: the all-wise and all-powerful Lord 'was his God by covenant and special promise, and fatherly affection.' Matthew Henry gives it the pastoral seal that the schools land on together. Where his men 'gave liberty to their passions,' David 'set his graces on work'; taking the LORD for his God, he could take encouragement from that relation 'in the worst of times,' assured that God 'can and will bring light out of darkness, peace out of trouble, and good out of evil' (Rom. 8:28). 'When he was at his wits' end,' Henry writes, 'he was not at his faith's end' — for what time I am afraid, I will trust in thee (Ps. 56:3).
7:
Encouraged in his God, David's first act is to send for the ephod, and the witnesses read the gesture as the turning of a soul from panic to counsel. The reformed Gill identifies the priest: Abiathar, son of that Ahimelech whom Saul had slaughtered at Nob, had fled to David with the ephod (1 Sam. 22:20; 1 Sam. 23:6) and now serves as high priest in his father's room. Gill marvels, with a touch of reproach, that David had not inquired of the LORD before ever setting out with Achish; 'perhaps the present disaster brought to mind that neglect, and made him the more diligent now.'

The puritan Matthew Henry makes that very neglect the lesson of the verse. It was a great advantage that David had the high priest and 'the breast-plate of judgment' to consult as a public person (Num. 27:21); yet when he went down among the Philistines he had not used it — 'perhaps he was ashamed to own his religion so far among the uncircumcised.' Now he apprehends that this trouble is sent 'to correct him for that oversight,' and Henry draws the general good: 'It is well if we get this good by our afflictions, to be reminded by them of neglected duties, and particularly to be quickened by them to enquire of the Lord' (1 Chron. 15:13). Though David's cause against Amalek was plainly just and his heart set on the rescue, still he would not stir without asking counsel, thereby owning his dependence upon God.

The wesleyan Wesley reads the priest's instruction with the same edge: bring me the ephod means 'put it upon thyself, that thou mayst enquire of God according to his ordinance,' for 'David was sensible of his former error in neglecting to ask counsel of God by the ephod, when he came to Achish.' His necessity now brings him to his duty, and his duty meets with success. Clarke weighs only the small question of who wore it — David himself, or, more likely, Abiathar at David's bidding. The lone dissent in emphasis is the grammatical-historical Ellicott, who is troubled by the whole silent stretch behind this verse: through all David's wanderings 'we hear nothing of prayer and of consultation of the Urim,' and the Philistine sojourn, undertaken 'entirely of himself' in distrust, had brought 'one unbroken series of sin and calamity.' Yet even Ellicott defends the reality of the oracle against the moderns who explain it away, insisting that 'in some way or other the Divine will was made known through the agency of the mysterious Urim and Thummim,' as the case of Saul, whom the LORD 'answered not by Urim' (1 Sam. 28:6), plainly assumes. The witnesses agree: affliction has driven David back to the neglected means of grace, and the ephod he had left silent now speaks.
8:
The inquiry and its answer are brief, and the witnesses dwell on the readiness of the God who replies. David asks two things — Shall I pursue after this troop? shall I overtake them? — and receives more than he asks: Pursue: for thou shalt surely overtake them, and without fail recover all. The reformed Gill notes only the mechanics, that the inquiry was made 'by Abiathar, who reported his questions to the Lord in his name,' the priest the appointed channel of the word.

The wesleyan Wesley draws out the tender particular that the others pass over: the manner of the answer. On earlier occasions, he recalls, 'God answered more slowly and gradually' — as at Keilah, where David had to ask twice (1 Sam. 23:11-12) — 'but now he answers speedily, and fully at once, because the business required haste.' From this Wesley lifts a doctrine of God's fatherly nearness: 'So gracious is our God, that he considers even the degree of our necessities, and accommodates himself to them.' The urgency of David's case is met by a corresponding swiftness in heaven; where the wives and children are being carried further off with every hour, the oracle does not delay or dole out its guidance piecemeal but gives the whole assurance in a breath.

Matthew Henry, whose comment gathers several verses at v.7, marks how the answer runs 'above what he asked' — for David inquired whether he might pursue and whether he might overtake, and God adds, unbidden, the promise that he 'should recover all.' Here the witnesses converge on the heart of the verse: the LORD does not merely permit the pursuit, he guarantees its issue. David is sent out not on a gamble but on a word, and the whole daring chase that follows — across the Besor, past the fainting two hundred, guided by a dying slave — rests on this single divine surely. The men who a few hours before spoke of stoning him will be led by a captain who now carries a certainty they cannot see. And the promise is comprehensive: not some, but all; not a remnant snatched from the fire, but every soul and every good restored. The verse teaches the witnesses what it taught David — that when we acknowledge God in our way, he does not answer grudgingly, but directs our steps and, in mercy, tells us the end from the beginning. What v.19 reports as fact, v.8 already holds as promise.
9:
The pursuit begins, and the witnesses read the march to the brook Besor for both its geography and its first hard test. David sets out with the six hundred men that were with him, and the reformed Gill notes the spring of their new resolve — they went 'encouraged by the oracle of the Lord,' the promise of v.8 turning a broken, mutinous band into a company on the hunt.

The grammatical-historical commentators map the ground and gauge the men. Clarke traces the brook Besor from its source 'in the mountain of Idumea' to its fall 'into the Mediterranean Sea beyond Gaza,' noting the old guess that it was one with the river of Egypt; there two hundred of the six 'were found so spent with fatigue that they could proceed no farther,' and were left to guard the baggage (v.24). Ellicott sharpens the picture of the force: the six hundred were no fresh levy but 'the old band of veteran soldiers, whose speed and endurance he could depend upon — men tried by many a weary night march.' Even of these, a large part could not stand the pace David set. Ellicott explains the collapse from the circumstances: the departure had been so hurried that 'but scanty provision for the forced march was made,' hence the falling out through weariness on the rapid advance; he identifies the Besor, tentatively, with the Wady Shariah below Askelon, while confessing it 'cannot be identified with certainty.' He notes, too, that the narrator has run ahead of himself — the remark for two hundred abode behind anticipates what v.10 will properly tell, 'a proleptical expression, arising from the vivacious description of David's rapid march.'

Matthew Henry, whose fuller comment stands at v.7, catches the resolve of a leader who 'went himself in person, and took with him all the force he had.' The witnesses set the scene without dispute: a spent and grieving army, freshly rallied by a word from God, presses south at a speed that only the hardiest can bear, until the brook Besor sifts the strong from the exhausted. The parting of the six hundred into four and two is not yet a matter of justice or reward — that will come at v.24 — but simply of strength; and already the reader sees that the recovery God has promised will be wrought not by overwhelming might but by a diminished and weary remnant, that the glory may be his.
10:
Two hundred fall out, and David presses on with four hundred, and the witnesses read the diminished band as a fresh trial of faith. The reformed Gill will not let the loss be minimized: David went on 'not discouraged with being obliged to leave a third part of his little army behind,' and this was 'doubtless a trial of his faith,' to pursue with so few an enemy whose numbers he did not know but must far exceed his own — for, as the sequel shows, 'as many escaped on camels as David had with him' (v.17). Four hundred men chasing a host that could lose four hundred and still be a host: the arithmetic is all against him.

The wesleyan Wesley states the same reckoning as a triumph of trust: 'Four hundred — a small number for such an attempt: but David was strong in faith, giving God the glory of his power and faithfulness.' The fewness of the force is not a weakness to be lamented but an occasion to lean wholly on the promise of v.8; the smaller the arm of flesh, the plainer it will be, at the victory, that the LORD gave the troop into his hand.

The puritan Matthew Henry, in his comment at v.7, draws two lessons from the parting at the Besor. The first is that very trial of faith: 'when we are disappointed and discouraged in our expectations from second causes, then to go on with cheerfulness, confiding in the divine power, this is giving glory to God, by believing against hope, in hope.' The second is a lesson in tenderness, for David 'would by no means urge them beyond their strength, though the case itself was so very urgent.' In this Henry sees a figure of Christ: 'The Son of David thus considers the frame of his followers, who are not all alike strong and vigorous in their spiritual pursuits and conflicts; but, where we are weak, there he is kind; nay, more, there he is strong' (2 Cor. 12:9-10). So the witnesses hold the two truths together without strain: David's faith presses forward with the four hundred who can go, and David's gentleness releases the two hundred who cannot — and both the pressing on and the letting go will be honoured before the day is done.
11:
Providence lays a stranger in the path, and the witnesses read the finding of the Egyptian as the hidden mechanism of the whole rescue. The men come upon him in the field, and the reformed Gill sketches his state — a man 'lying there, having been sick, and was half starved, almost dead.' That David's four hundred, straining after a vanished enemy with no notion of his route, should stumble on the one living creature able to guide them is, to the witnesses, no accident at all.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott fills in who the man was and why he lay dying. The Amalekites were 'a nomad race' whose wanderings reached 'the frontiers of Egypt, hence the probability of their having Egyptian slaves in their tribe.' He returns to their character — 'a ruthless, cruel race, the scourge of the desert' — and fastens on the barbarity of the present case: since 'they had evidently many camels in their force' (v.17), 'the abandonment of the sick slave, left, without food or water, to die of hunger, was a needless act of barbarity on their part.' They had beasts to carry him and chose instead to leave him to the vultures.

The puritan Matthew Henry, commenting at v.7, turns the whole episode into a study of God's justice and David's mercy. God, he says, 'chooses the foolish things of the world, with them to confound the wise': a poor, cast-off Egyptian lad becomes the instrument of a great deliverance. The master's cruelty in leaving a spent servant 'to perish in the field' Henry brands the mark of an Amalekite and not an Israelite — 'the tender mercies of the wicked are cruel' (Prov. 12:10) — and he sees a sharp retribution in it, that 'God hears the cry of oppressed servants' and made this abused slave 'instrumental towards the destruction of a whole army of Amalekites and his master among the rest.' Against that cruelty he sets David's compassion: though the lad may well have been one of those who helped burn Ziklag, 'finding him in distress, he generously relieved him' with bread and water, refusing to let one 'drawn unto death' go unhelped (Prov. 24:11-12). The witnesses agree in the wonder of it — the very cruelty of the strong casts a weapon into the hands of the merciful, and a kindness shown to a dying nobody opens the door to the rescue of all.
12:
David's men revive the dying man with food, and the witnesses read the small kindness as the pivot on which the pursuit turns. They give him a piece of a cake of figs, and two clusters of raisins, and the reformed Gill pauses even on the fare — figs 'that were dried and pressed together, and made into cakes,' the concentrated traveller's food of that country; Clarke simply refers back to the like provision Abigail once brought (1 Sam. 25:18). When he had eaten, his spirit came again to him, for he had tasted nothing three days and three nights.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative forward through the revived man's report: asked whence he came, he owns himself 'a young man of Egypt, servant to an Amalekite,' left behind sick 'three days agone,' and he tells his rescuers that his band had 'invaded the south of the Crethites, and what belongs to Judah, and the south of Caleb, and burned Ziklag with fire.' Ellicott presses the strategic weight of the timing: the note of 'three days and three nights' measures 'the amount of start the Amalekite leader with the plunder had,' and the point is urgent, for the raiders' lead was very considerable. Here the witnesses see the moral turn of the whole story: 'The cruelty of the Amalekites to their slaves,' Ellicott writes, 'was the cause of their ultimate discomfiture, for with the very considerable start they already had, if David had not been quite certain, through the information of the Egyptian, of their route, the pursuit would have been utterly hopeless.'

So the two clusters of raisins are, in the witnesses' reading, worth an army. The same barbarity that cast the slave aside to die now delivers his masters into David's hand, and the same compassion that stooped to feed a stranger now purchases the one thing four hundred weary men most need — knowledge of where the enemy has gone. The verse joins mercy and providence in a single act: what Israel gives out of pity, God turns into the very means of victory. A man three days from bread is set upright by a handful of dried fruit, and with him rises the whole hope of Ziklag's captives, for the tongue that thanks his deliverers in v.13 will point the way to v.16, where the spoilers lie feasting and unaware.
13:
David questions the man he has revived, and the witnesses read his answer as the unveiling of a providence long prepared. To David's To whom belongest thou? and whence art thou? the man replies that he is a young Egyptian, servant to an Amalekite, whose master 'left me, because three days agone I fell sick.' The reformed Gill lets the bare exchange stand — 'To what country or people? and to whom among them?' — the opening of the interrogation on which everything now hangs.

The wesleyan voices draw the doctrine of overruling providence in the man's very nationality. Wesley marvels that God 'so ordering it, that he was not one of that cursed race of the Amalekites, who were to be utterly destroyed, but an Egyptian, who might be spared.' Had he been an Amalekite, the ban would have forbidden David to preserve him; being an Egyptian, he could be shown mercy and made a guide. And Wesley presses the master's cruelty into the same frame: to leave a sick servant 'in this place and condition' was 'barbarous inhumanity,' for he might easily have carried him off with the plunder; 'but he paid dear for this cruelty, for this was the occasion of the ruin of him and all their company. And God by his secret providence ordered the matter thus for that very end.' From it Wesley lifts a maxim: 'there is no fighting against God, who can make the smallest accidents serviceable to the production of the greatest effects.' Clarke says the same in his blunter key — the abandonment 'was very inhuman: though they had booty enough, and no doubt asses sufficient to carry the invalids, yet they left this poor man to perish; and God visited it upon them, as he made this very person the means of their destruction.'

The witnesses thus find in the sick slave's confession the whole logic of the chapter in miniature. A pagan master's callousness becomes the hinge of his own doom; a nationality that spares the man from Israel's ban becomes the credential that lets him serve; and a question casually put to a rescued stranger opens straight upon the enemy's camp. Twice over the point is that God governs the least things — the falling sick of a slave, the accident of his birth, the barbarity of his abandonment — and bends them all toward the deliverance he has promised. What men do in cruelty and what men do in mercy alike run in the channel God has cut, so that the deserted Egyptian, worthless in his master's eyes, is precisely the instrument heaven has reserved to guide David to v.15 and the recovery of all.
14:
The Egyptian describes the raid in full, and the witnesses turn chiefly to the peoples he names — the south of the Cherethites, the coast of Judah, and the south of Caleb — with a long antiquarian interest in the first. The reformed Gill and Wesley agree in the main: the Cherethites are 'the Philistines,' or one southern nation of them, and 'Caleb' is added by way of explanation, meaning that part of the south of Judah belonging to Caleb's posterity. Keil and Delitzsch anchor the terms in Scripture, reading the Cherethites (with Ezek. 25:16 and Zeph. 2:5) as the Philistine tribes of the south-west, and the south of Caleb as the country round Hebron.

The grammatical-historical Clarke and Ellicott expand the note into history. Clarke, following Calmet, presses the reading 'Creti,' holding the Cherethites to be 'aborigines of Crete, from which they had their name,' the people over whom Zephaniah and Ezekiel pronounce woe, and the same who later 'formed a part of David's guards' (2 Sam. 15:18); the south of Caleb he places about Kirjath-arba and Kirjath-sepher. Ellicott makes the fullest survey, calling the whole history of this 'singular Philistine people' most obscure — settlers, not natives, of Canaan, who gave their name Palestine to the land; he links their origin to Caphtor, the Philistine home (Amos 9:7), 'not improbably identical with Crete,' and notes the fresh arrivals 'from Crete and other western regions' before Samson's time. He too traces the Cherethites forward: enrolled, he thinks, during David's Ziklag days as the nucleus of the foreign body-guard he kept 'about his person all through his reign' under Benaiah — a practice he illustrates from the Scottish archers, the Swiss Guards of the French kings, and the Varangian guard of the Greek emperors. The 'coast which belongeth to Judah' he explains as the eastern Negeb reaching to the Dead Sea, and the south of Caleb as the district given the faithful spy round Hebron.

Beneath the geography the witnesses hear the plain confession that closes the verse: and we burned Ziklag with fire. Ellicott reads it as the deliberate climax of the raid — 'a piece of stern revenge for the late incursion of David into their country, and for the cruelties practised on the captives.' The Egyptian's precise itinerary is more than colour; it is the map by which David will overtake the enemy, and it confirms that the blow at Ziklag was aimed, of all the plundered towns, at David himself. The very thoroughness of Amalek's vengeance is now, in a servant's mouth, the intelligence that will undo it.
